Folding a pair in this spot would be overly conservative. I'll call and see the river in position.

River 8d completes the diamond flush (board now has three diamonds: 7d, 9d, 8d, so any single diamond completes a flush). My top pair top kicker? No, top pair nines with 6 kicker is vulnerable to Ax, flushes (Adx, Kdx, QJx suited diamonds, etc.), straights (J10 for 7-8-9-10-J), sets, and two pairs (A9, A8, 98, 75, etc.). Preflop limp-call from SB wide range included many speculative hands and Ax that called small flop/turn bets. Betting risks getting called/raised only by better hands while folding out worse pairs/draws/bluffs that check behind. Pot control with showdown value against possible Ax, worse 9x, or missed draws is priority OOP. Checking induces bluffs from missed draws/overcards and allows value from worse if Claude bets light, while I can call reasonably sized bets (~1/2 pot) given equity and history of Claude calling stations but also folding to aggression. Stacks deep (pot 1050, villain 15.8k), preserve upside heads-up late (138/150). No need to bluff as first to act; rep strength from prior bets.

Elon checked the river on a completed board (As-7d-9d-5c-8d), indicating weakness or showdown mentality. I hold a pair of sevens (7c with 7d on the board), which is a respectable made hand that beats many unpaired hands and weaker pairs in Elon's likely range from the big blind. Betting risks losing to better holdings or being called only by superior hands, while checking allows me to realize my hand's showdown value safely. With only 12 hands remaining in the tournament and my stack slightly short of Elon's, minimizing unnecessary risk while maintaining chip position is strategically sound. Checking to showdown is the mathematically and positionally appropriate play.

Heads-up on the button/SB with Q2o, a marginal but playable hand in this spot due to position. In heads-up play, the SB should raise a very wide range (often 100% or close) to steal blinds and apply pressure, especially with deep stacks (241BB effective). Historical play shows Claude folds frequently to preflop raises (e.g., Hands 50, 54, 61, 89, etc.), and I have successfully raised light many times. Limping is passive and invites trouble postflop with poor playability; folding loses the SB EV. Standard min-raise to 3x BB (300) balances stealing and value while denying equity realization. This maximizes fold equity and position advantage late in the tournament.

K8o is a reasonable hand in heads-up play with good pot odds (2:1). In a 2-player scenario late in the tournament with relatively balanced stacks, calling to see the flop is the optimal play. Folding is too passive against Elon's frequent raising pattern, while raising with K8o is unnecessarily aggressive. The hand has decent equity and playability post-flop.

K8o makes top pair on a Ks Jh Kd flop. I'm out of position and should take the initiative rather than check and face pressure. Betting 350 (roughly 58% of the 600 pot) is a balanced sizing that: (1) extracts value from Elon's weaker holdings and Broadway hands, (2) applies pressure while not overcommitting with a weak kicker, and (3) allows me to gather information on his hand strength. In heads-up play late in the tournament with reasonable stack depth, aggression with top pair is the correct approach.

Facing a flop of Ks Jh Kd, a paired kings board that heavily favors villain's BB calling range over my light Button/SB steal-raise range. My 2h Qc has zero equity: no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (queen-high with jack blocker but no gutshot even). Claude's 350 bet into 950 pot (~38% pot) is small, possibly weak like a jack, low pocket, or pure float/blocker, but even weak hands beat me, and any king/Jx/set/straight draw crushes. Calling builds pot OOP with air, no fold equity later. Raising has no showdown value and risks stacks on bluff into a capping range. Folding preserves deep stack (238BB eff.) for better spots in final hands of tournament. History shows Claude calls light but donks here indicate strength or semi-bluff we dominate anyway.

Calling with Q9o from button heads-up. Hand has reasonable equity in heads-up play, and position postflop is decisive. Cost is negligible (0.3% of stack) for a chance to play in position against Elon's aggression. Standard exploitative play in this stack depth.

Heads-up in the late stages (hand 140/150), with a larger stack (23,775 vs Claude's 16,025). Holding Ad Jc in BB vs SB/Button limp (wide range likely including many dominated hands and speculative calls). AJ offsuit is a premium hand heads-up, with good equity, straight potential, and domination potential vs Ax, Kx, suited connectors. Raising isolates, builds the pot for value, takes initiative on a dry board potential, and applies pressure. Standard size: 3x BB (300) is appropriate without overcommitting stacks.

Calling with Q9o from button heads-up. After raising to 300, I need to call 200 more to reach the 300 total. Q9o has solid ~50-55% equity against Elon's range, position is decisive postflop, and pot odds of 2.4:1 are favorable. Standard exploitative play in heads-up at this stack depth where button must defend wider ranges. Total commitment: 300 to match the current bet.

Heads-up on the flop with nut flush draw (Ad) on a monochromatic diamond board (4d7dQd). Jc provides some overcard value but primary strength is the 9 nut outs (~35% equity vs. typical range) plus fold equity. Claude's limp-call range from SB/Button is wide: suited aces (possibly worse flush draws), pocket pairs, suited connectors, broadway. Pre-flop raise gives me initiative and strong range rep. Checking risks free cards to his draws/improvers and misses value. Betting semi-bluffs: extracts from worse draws/pairs, protects, builds pot for when we hit. Size: ~2/3 pot (400) balances value, protection, and pot odds denial without overbetting vs. shorter stack. Late in tournament (140/150), apply pressure to grow stack.

Calling the flop bet with Qs9c on a three-diamond board (4d7dQd). Top pair is decent but vulnerable to flush draws. Calling allows me to reassess on the turn when the board dynamics become clearer. Position on the button provides postflop control. Stack depth (15.8k vs 23.2k) and board texture make calling prudent—raising commits too many chips to a vulnerable hand without strong reason, while calling lets the turn card determine whether my equity has improved or deteriorated relative to Elon's range.

Heads-up on turn with nut flush draw (Ad) on a scary diamond flop that Claude called my continuation bet into. The 2c is a complete blank, not completing any straight draws or helping obvious hands, keeping my 9 nut outs (~18% equity standalone, more with fold equity). Claude's limp-call preflop and flop call range includes worse flush draws (e.g., Kd, Td, 8d5d), Qx (top pair), smaller pairs, and bluffs/missed draws, but unlikely to have the nuts given action. I have position advantage effectively through initiative, larger stack (23k vs 15k), and late in tournament (140/150) - need to apply pressure to chip up. Checking risks giving a free card to his draws or inducing check-raises from monsters, while pot control is less important with the draw. Betting semi-bluffs for value from worse hands/draws, protection, and fold equity vs. his medium strength. Size ~2/3 pot (900) balances range, denies odds to draws (~2:1 pot odds if calling), and commits him relatively without overbetting effective stacks.

Folding top pair on a three-diamond board where I have zero diamond backup. Elon's raise-flop-raise-turn pattern on this board strongly indicates a flush or strong hand. My Qs9c has weak equity against his range—likely 12-15% if he has a flush, 45% if he has overpair, but his line suggests flush-heavy holdings. Three diamonds already on board makes my non-diamond top pair extremely vulnerable. With 10 hands remaining and limited chips, preserving stack for better spots is strategically sound. Fold.

Heads-up on the button/SB with Ad8s, an ace-high offsuit hand with decent playability and a strong blocker. Position post-flop is excellent as button acts last. Pot odds to call are great (calling 50 into 200), but raising builds the pot with a strong range advantage heads-up, where A8o is well above calling range. Villain Claude has been aggressive pre-flop but folds frequently to 3-bets and post-flop aggression in recent hands (e.g., Hand 140 folded turn, Hand 139 folded river). Standard play is to raise 3x BB to 300, putting pressure while maintaining fold equity and initiative. Deep stacks allow post-flop flexibility. Near end of tournament (141/150), aim to accumulate chips without high variance spots.

3-7 offsuit is the worst hand in poker with virtually no value. Even though we're in a heads-up situation on the button/BB, there's no mathematical justification to play this hand. Folding preserves our 15k+ stack (which is healthy at 50/100 blinds) for better opportunities in the remaining 9 hands.

Playing 5d9s on the button (small blind) in heads-up play near the end of the tournament (hand 142 of 150). This is a weak hand, but in heads-up poker, ranges are significantly wider than full-ring games. With ~76 BB effective stack depth, calling the 50 additional chips to see a cheap flop is reasonable. I have position postflop and can evaluate my hand based on board texture. Folding is too tight here, and raising would be overcommitting with a marginal hand. The pot odds (3:1 to call) make calling a reasonable play to take a flop and apply position-based play postflop.

Heads-up in late position (BB vs SB limp). My hand 2s9d is very weak: low, offsuit, poor playability, dominated in most scenarios. Pot is 200, I've posted BB, to call 0. No need to raise as a bluff here with trash; better to check and realize equity on flop for cheap. If flop misses (likely ~70%+ time), can check-fold. With deep stacks and tournament nearing end, preserve stack rather than spew chips preflop. Checking keeps pot small and gives chance for miracle flop.

Heads-up on the flop in BB vs SB limp. My 2s9d has zero equity: no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw, gutshot nothing relevant. Board Ah8d6d is coordinated with flush draw and possible straights (7-5, 9-7, etc.). Claude's limping range hits this flop decently often (Ax, 87, 76, diamond draws, etc.) and can have strong hands. Pot is small at 200, stacks deep but tournament endgame favors preservation. Betting is spew as a bluff (he calls/raises light due to position and board texture). Check-fold is optimal to realize 0 equity and avoid tough river spots.

I'm in heads-up play near the end of the tournament (Hand 142 of 150) with 5d9s on the button against Elon who just checked. My hand is very weak - essentially 9-high with minimal equity on this Ah 8d 6d flop. I have no made hand, no flush draw (only one diamond), and no meaningful straight draw. The three-diamond flop is dangerous; any diamond gives my opponent a flush draw if they don't already have one. Since Elon checked, he likely either completely missed or has a marginal hand himself. Checking here is the correct play because: (1) it minimizes losses with a very weak hand, (2) it lets me see the turn card cheaply to potentially improve or gain information, and (3) with ~76 BB effective stacks, preserving chips is important in the endgame. If Elon bets on future streets, I'll have better information for decision-making.

Heads-up pot of 200 on a draw-heavy board (Ah8d6d2d) with three diamonds. My 2s9d improved to bottom pair (pairing the deuce) plus a strong flush draw (9d plus three board diamonds, any diamond on river gives flush). Claude limped pre (wide range including Ax, suited connectors, draws) and checked flop, suggesting medium strength or missed draws. In late tournament (142/150), with deeper stack (24k vs 15k effective), I have excellent equity (~40-50% vs calling range) and fold equity on scary board. Semi-bluff betting protects hand, denies equity to his draws/one-pair, and gets value from worse Ax/pairs/draws. Small sizing ~3/4 pot (150) balances thin value and pot control, avoiding overcommitment while building pot if called. Checking risks giving free card to draws or induces bluff but realizes too much equity passively; betting maximizes EV.

With 9-high on a four-diamond board (Ah 8d 6d 2d), my hand has essentially zero showdown value. Elon's turn bet after checking the flop suggests he has developed some hand strength—either a made hand, a flush draw that completed, or a strong semi-bluff. Even accounting for bluffing frequency in heads-up play, I have virtually no equity here. My hand cannot win against any reasonable holding from Elon's range, and I have no draws to improve. With 150 to call into a 350 pot, I would need improbable fold equity or substantial equity on my hand to justify the call. Neither condition is met. In the endgame of a tournament (hand 142/150) with limited chips remaining, folding weak hands like 9-high with no draws is mathematically required. Preserving chips for situations where I have legitimate equity is critical.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with deep stacks and 8 hands left. J4o is playable from this position due to wide button opening ranges (~50-60% of hands). Position postflop allows me to realize equity well. Claude in BB will fold often to a standard raise, stealing the 150 pot frequently. If called, hand has straight draw potential and jack blocker. Standard raise sizing 3x BB to 300 total. Maximizes EV by balancing steals with value.

Folding Q6o. Out of position against an aggressive raise with a weak, unconnected hand. With 7 hands remaining and adequate chip stack, the correct strategy is to preserve chips and wait for premium holdings in favorable positions rather than gambling with marginal hands. This is a standard fold.

In heads-up play on the button with K7o, raising is the optimal strategy. While K7o is not a premium hand, in heads-up situations the button range widens significantly, and we gain substantial positional advantage postflop. The math supports action: we only need ~20% equity to call, and K7o performs better than that in heads-up scenarios. 

A raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is standard and allows us to:
1. Apply pressure and maintain initiative
2. Extract value from button position
3. Potentially win the pot preflop
4. Control the action postflop if called

We're in the final stretch (hand 144/150) with a chip deficit (38% vs 62%), so aggressive but measured plays on the button are crucial. A raise is superior to both calling and folding in this spot.

Hand #144, late in tournament (144/150). In BB with 24,775 stack vs Claude's 14,875 on button/SB. He raised to 250 (2.5x), pot 350, to call 150 more (total 250 committed). T8o is a marginal but playable defending hand vs wide button range in BB. Good pot odds (350:150 ~2.3:1 direct, better implied OOP deep). Straight potential, live cards. Opponent aggressive from history (frequent raises/calls but folds to pressure). Calling to realize equity, control pot OOP, see flop cheaply. Folding too tight late; raising too strong for this hand. Plan: Call, play postflop cautiously based on board texture.

Flop: 9d Jd 3h gives me an open-ended straight draw (8-T-9-J, needs 7 or Q) with ~32% equity against a typical button c-bet range (strong vs bluffs, decent vs value). No pair or flush draw, but live cards and deep effective stacks (he has ~15bb? No, stacks 24k/14k at 50/100 blinds? Blinds escalated? Anyway, pot 500 small relative). OOP first to act postflop after defending wide pre. Leading (donk betting) with pure draw is suboptimal OOP as it risks folding to raise and denies equity realization. Checking standard: induces wide c-bet from aggressive button raiser (Claude often bets flops), allows check-raise semi-bluff if he bets small, or cheap call with implied odds if he bets pot. Late tournament (144/150), preserve stack, realize equity multiway impossible (HU). Folding equity high if we raise later streets. Plan: Check, evaluate his bet size/range, call medium bets/extract on turn/river if improve or he weak.

Continuation bet with King-high and flush draw on a checked flop. Elon's check suggests a weak range, and my hand has solid equity with both top pair and four flush outs. Standard 2/3 pot bet to maintain aggression, build the pot, and apply pressure while not overcommitting in the final stretch of the tournament.

Flop c-bet of 350 into 850 pot (~41% pot) from Claude on 9dJd3h board. With Tc8h, I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs to Broadway: 7 or Q, ~16% turn, 32% by river) plus gutshot elements and backdoor flush potential. Pot odds excellent: calling 350 into 850 gives ~29% required equity, which I exceed vs his wide button range (frequent preflop raiser from history). Implied odds strong with his 14.5k stack behind and my deeper stack for value if I hit. Raising semi-bluff risks folding him out cheaply or facing shove OOP late tournament; check-raise was option pre-action but call induces thin value or allows free turn if he checks behind later. History shows Claude aggressive but folds to pressure (e.g., Hands 13,14,91); preserve fold equity by calling small bet, control pot OOP, realize equity deepstacked near end (144/150). Plan: Call, check turn, barrel/draw if improves or he weak.

Turn board Ad completes the nut flush draw (diamonds) and adds straight possibilities (e.g., Q10, KT), a scary card that likely improves strong hands or hits draws from Claude's button raising range. I hold Tc8h with a clean open-ended straight draw (8 outs to Broadway: 4x7, 4xQ ~17% equity to river) but no pair, flush draw, or other made hand. OOP first to act into pot of 1200 with deep effective stacks (Claude 14.5k). Betting a pure draw OOP is poor: denies equity by folding out worse hands cheaply, risks raise/shove from flushes/top pair/sets. Checking preserves stack late in tournament (144/150), induces bluffs/bets from his wide polarized range (continues with flush, strong pairs, or semi-bluffs; checks weak air), allows cheap realization of equity with good implied odds if I hit. History shows Claude aggressive on flops but can fold to pressure; check keeps fold equity alive for potential check-raise bluff on river or if he overbets. Plan: Check, call medium bets with 17%+ equity/odds, fold to shoves, check-raise large bets semi-bluff if represents flush well. Maximizes EV by controlling pot OOP and avoiding thin value spew.

Elon has checked both the flop and turn, indicating a weak hand or air. With K♦ 7♠ on a 9♦ J♦ 3♥ A♦ board, I have K-high (strong for heads-up) plus 9 diamond outs to make the nuts or near-nuts flush. A 500 chip bet (roughly 40% pot) applies appropriate pressure without overcommitting given my chip disadvantage. This builds the pot when I have equity, potentially wins it now against weakness, and positions me well for the river. In the final 7 hands with inferior chips, aggressive but measured betting on draws with solid current equity is optimal.

Turn Ad completes nut flush and improves straight draws, polarizing Claude's button range to flushes, strong Ax/value, or bluffs continuing aggression (history: frequent flop bets but folds to river pressure, e.g., Hands 91,140). Holding Tc8h OESD (8 outs to Broadway ~17% river equity), no showdown. OOP into 1700 pot facing 500 bet (~29% pot): direct odds 1700:500=3.4:1 (~23% needed), favorable vs his wide polarized range (equity ~25-35% vs bluffs/value mix). Implied odds strong with his 14k stack (hit Broadway nuts/draw-crusher). Raising semi-bluff risks fold-out worse or shove from flush OOP late (144/150, preserve fold equity river); folding nitty given odds/history (he bets weak flops). Calling controls pot, realizes equity cheaply deep effective, plan check-river/call small or fold large if miss (barrel if improves/shove). Maximizes EV preserving stack endgame.

River King of clubs is a blank that doesn't pair the board or complete obvious draws beyond what's already possible. Holding Tc8h, I have no pair, no flush, and completely missed my flop open-ended straight draw (needing 7 or Q). Pure air with zero showdown value. Claude's line (PFR button raise, flop c-bet on draw-heavy 9dJd3h, turn bet 500/1200 ~42% pot after Ad flush-completes) strongly reps value: nut diamond flushes (Adx, JdTd, etc.), strong Ax (AJ+, AKo/s), top pair good kicker, sets/two-pair, or occasional thin value/ semi-bluffs but polarized heavy on value given turn bet sizing and board. History shows Claude aggressive pre/flop but folds to postflop pressure (e.g., Hands 13,91,140 where he folds to turns/rivers); however, OOP first to act river deepstacked (eff. 14k into 2200 pot late 144/150), betting/raising bluff is -EV: villain calls profitably with better (his value range crushes), small bets get called/folded to equally well by checks, denies thin value extraction if he checks weak. Checking standard OOP with air: induces wide value/thin bets from his range (he often fires multiple streets), high fold equity if check-raise but unnecessary risk OOP vs strong rep'd range; preserves stack endgame, realizes 0 equity optimally by folding to any bet. No merit to hero call range or bluff here—max stack by prudent fold.

River value bet with top pair. Elon checked flop, turn, and river - a clear indication of weakness. With K♦7♠ making a pair of Kings on a 9♦J♦3♥A♦K♣ board, this is the strongest likely holding. A 600 chip bet (27% pot) extracts value from Elon's weaker hands while not overcommitting with only 7 hands remaining and a chip deficit. This measured aggression is optimal for the tournament situation.

River Kc blank. Missed OESD completely (Tc8h): air, no pair/flush/straight/showdown value. Claude's button PFR + 3-street betting line (flop 350/850~41%, turn 500/1700~29% on Ad flush-completer, river 600/2800~21%) reps massive strength: diamond flushes (C(10,2)=45 combos needing 2 diamonds), AJs/KQs broadway, sets (99/JJ/33/AA ~12), J9/J3/A9/A3 two-pair, AJ+ top pair. Turn Ad bet kills most missed draws (only stubborn floats/semis continue, rare). Small river bet induces raises from draws/pairs or extracts thin vs weaker. Pot odds ~600:2800 (3400 total, ~15% needed) but equity ~10-20% max vs polarized (bluff-heavy estimate optimistic; history shows Claude aggro pre/flop but value-oriented multi-street, e.g., Hands 39,45,94 folds to pressure but bets strong boards). HU late (144/150, my 23775~238BB vs his 13425~134BB), ICM favors folding air to preserve deep stack for superior positions/exploits in final 6 hands. Hero-call spews; fold optimal OOP realization (0 equity cheaply).

AJs is a premium hand heads-up, strong equity against Claude's BB defending range. Acting first pre-flop from SB/Button, standard to raise for value and initiative, as we have position post-flop. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) to build pot with strong hand while denying equity to weaker hands. Deep stacks allow flexibility post-flop. Late tournament, but chips in play justify aggression to maximize stack.

In heads-up play with 5 hands remaining, I need to defend my big blind with reasonable holdings. Ac5d has decent equity against Elon's wide button-raising range and plays reasonably postflop. With a 2:1 pot odds and meaningful equity, calling is the standard play here rather than folding or re-raising with such a weak kicker.

With Ac5d on 8s 6c Td in heads-up endgame (5 hands remaining), I check after Elon's flop check. While I have ace-high, the weak kicker and lack of draws makes betting marginal. Checking controls pot size with a weak hand, gathers information on the turn, and maintains flexibility. The dry board and Elon's check suggest both of us have modest holdings - proceeding cautiously is prudent.

Heads-up on the button with position post-flop, AJhh is a strong pre-flop raising hand. Flop 8s6cTd is rainbow and somewhat coordinated (straight draws like J9, 97, 75 possible), but misses me completely. However, standard to c-bet here for 2/3 pot (400 into 600) as continuation bet to maintain initiative, deny equity to draws/weak pairs in Claude's wide BB defending range, and leverage fold equity. AJ blocks strong Ax and some straight combos. Backdoor flush draw adds some equity. Deep stacks (23k effective) allow for post-flop flexibility if called. Late tournament pressure favors aggression to build stack. Claude checked, indicating weakness. Betting extracts value from worse or takes it down.

With Ac5d on 8s 6c Td, I'm facing a flop bet out of position with a weak holding. In heads-up endgame (5 hands remaining), Ac5d lacks the equity or positional advantage to justify calling. Elon's bet suggests a real holding rather than a bluff. I have adequate chips to be selective, so folding and waiting for higher-quality spots in the final hands is the stronger long-term strategy.

A8o is a solid heads-up hand, and I'm on the button with positional advantage. With only 4 hands left in the tournament, I need to accumulate chips through selective aggression. Raising to 200 (standard raise) takes the initiative and puts pressure on Elon in the big blind. This hand is strong enough to play aggressively, and my position allows me to control the action postflop if called.

Heads-up with 5 hands remaining, deep stacks (effective 157BB). Button/SB Claude raised to 200, a wide range expected (~40-50% hands). My 9h4d offsuit is trash: low equity (~30-35% vs wide range), poor playability (no straight/flush potential), dominated by any 9x or high cards. Facing OOP postflop, calling 100 into 300 (3:1 odds) leads to difficult spots where I check-fold most flops. Raising as bluff not ideal with this hand lacking blockers or postflop potential. Folding preserves stack for premium spots to maximize endgame EV.

Hand #147, pre-flop, heads-up on the button/SB with 4h Ts (offsuit, gapped, weak hand). Pot 150, to call 50 (3:1 odds), but post-flop OOP with poor playability, no strong draws or equity. Claude has folded to many raises in history and shows aggression but exploitable. Near tournament end (hand 147/150), preserve stack for better spots rather than spew chips OOP with trash. Optimal: fold the SB.

Folding Q2o (Queen-deuce offsuit). While button position normally justifies wider ranges in heads-up play, Q2o is simply too weak of a hand. It has poor showdown value, limited playability post-flop, and will be out of position against any aggressive action. With only 2 hands left in the tournament and a relatively tight chip position (16,025 vs 23,825), preserving chips with weak holdings is the correct approach. Folding a weak hand from the button is standard poker."

Heads-up on the button/SB with A2o preflop. Ace-high hands are strong in heads-up play due to wide ranges. Excellent position acting first, but we can raise to steal the BB or build a pot with domination potential. Stacks are deep (mine 24k, villain 16k), so standard raise sizing of 3x BB (300) is appropriate to apply pressure late in the tournament with only 2 hands left. Folding or limping is too passive and misses value.

Folding 8-5o in the big blind vs. a button raise. While short-stacked in a heads-up scenario, this hand is too weak with insufficient equity even against the 2:1 pot odds. With one final hand remaining after this, it's better to preserve chips and play selectively. Elon will win this pot, and I'll face the final hand with slightly diminished but still reasonable chip stack.

Final hand of the tournament with a premium holding (Ac Td). In heads-up play on the button, this is a strong hand that warrants aggression. I'm raising to 250 chips total (a 200-chip raise) to build the pot and apply pressure. This sizing is standard and allows me to maintain good value while building equity in the pot for this premium starting hand. The goal is to win chips while maintaining appropriate risk/reward ratios with strong hands.

9h2c is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Facing a raise from the button/SB, who often opens wide, but this hand performs terribly post-flop OOP, with minimal equity against any reasonable calling range and horrible reverse implied odds. Pot odds are about 2.3:1 (needing ~30% equity), but 92o has far less against a polarized button range. Folding preserves my healthy 24BB stack (23k/100) heading into the final hand, avoiding unnecessary variance in a spot where I'm crushed.
